speaker: interviewer
text: --ghetto, where did you get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the Germans-- I don't know, there was a bakery somewhere. And they baked some sort of bread, which was bad. But they gave for each person-- can't remember how much bread. You know, a portion of bread every day. You had to go and get it somewhere. As you know, we had Ukrainians all around us. Some of-- some were not too bad. So we used to barter with them, give them if we still had something left over, you know, whatever, like clothes or-- money wasn't worth anything-- maybe a piece of jewelry, or something like that, or some household item.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to-- they used to bring into the ghetto. That was in the beginning. They used to bring in potatoes, maybe a few eggs, something like that. Some people used to go outside of the ghetto and bring in a few things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, it wasn't that strict. You could get out once in a while. But later on, you couldn't move. They were-- we were just surrounded. Like leaving that, you couldn't move anywhere. And we were hungry. We were starving. Some people did starve. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for sanitary arrangements, heating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. Very bad. In our case, I think we lived in the ghetto only one winter. And in our case, we were lucky, because we had lots of trees in our yard. So we used to chop up some trees. People took apart fences. You know, you did whatever you could to survive. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food? What did you cook beside the bread you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not very much. You know, as I say, once in a while, a bit of potato or a few beans. Not much. We didn't have very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have holidays while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The first year, we observed just about every holiday. We had-- it was just a few houses past our house. It was a big house. And the people made a meal in there. And people used to go and pray. They closed off all the windows and everything, used to go and pray. I remember exactly the first Rosh Hashanah in the ghetto. And then we still had a little bit of food. And I remember my mother making some, I think, maybe chicken soup even. And you know, shutting all the windows, closing it up, making dark so nobody sees you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody sees that you're praying and have candles.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we did have-- we observed, as bad as things were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We couldn't have any matzah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day? Did you go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the  ghetto, you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's where I worked is with this uncle in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the fur business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And also, they had-- they needed wool, the Germans, to make wool, because it was so Cold. They were freezing. So we learned to spin wool. Some people knew those things, and then they set up spinning wheels, and they taught us how to do it. And we used to spin wool. Other women were knitting, you know, and sort of things like that. I mean, we didn't have fun, believe me. But we did have-- you know, we were sort of a comara-- camaraderie--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, camaraderie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard to say. We-- we had that. We used to talk a lot with each other. We used to dream of things. What are we going to do, how are we going to do it? A lot of people had hope to run away, to plot, to do things. And as you'll see later when I tell you, some people did run away from the ghetto, and they did save themselves. And so we were doing all those things. And of course, we still had some books to read. But we used to talk a lot, discuss things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any news from the rest of the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the world? Nothing, nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no underground radio or newspaper, leaflets?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have anything like that. We were just too small. You know, radio just started coming in in those days just before the war. It wasn't developed like it is here, so we didn't have any news. But everybody used to think and wonder why the Germans are so mean to us. What did we ever do to them to be-- and they were all hoping that Roosevelt-- well, they remembered him, that he was the president-- that he won't let, after all, everybody die, that he will send some help. But of course, never came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was still a lot of hope at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was hope, ever time-- to the last minute, I think, there was hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? Was the ghetto liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it, came in [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] spring. It was Shabbat, I remember that. I think it was '42, maybe. And what happened then, they went in. The SS came in the middle of night, and they went to every house. They picked out whoever they saw and took them away and killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- took them away where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took them-- in our case, it was outside of town, a few miles outside. They made them dig the graves. And then they let them stand on the end of the road, and they shot them. I don't remember how many people. I don't think that there is a statistic about that. But as I say, everybody that they found, they did. In mine case, my family, we went down to our cellar with the other occupants of the house, and we all sat there. They walked into the house. The house was empty. They didn't look too close, and they didn't found us. And the next day, you could walk out, and you were free. And nothing happened to you. They were just a fast [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the way you-- it means. And then we survived this whole summer yet. Of course, we found who was killed. I mean, it was terrible for us to see what family, friends-- but nevertheless, we survived, and we kept on living till the same year in the fall, in Sukkot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in the ghetto setting? Were you guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- and from then on, we were really guarded. You couldn't get out. You couldn't get in. You couldn't-- they didn't even want you to work anymore. You were, like, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just kept.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just sat there and wait for them to come and to kill you. So we lived there till fall. And in fall was the liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in still in '42, the fall of '42. I mean, things happened so fast. It only took months, sometimes days. So-- so they surrounded us. And they also took young, strong men to dig graves the night before. And we knew that that's the end of us. We really knew. It was-- now that I can think of it, it was so sad that here you are, young people, old people, children, that you know, for what reason you should-- you have to die. And I must say that the will to live was so strong in everybody. And me, I think I just wanted to live. I almost didn't care about anything else. But I wanted to save myself. But there was no way to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents-- not my father and my brothers, but my mother, myself, and my sister-- a neighbor, they had a bakery, like, they run a bakery years ago. And then they had a big chimney there. I'm not sure exactly how it was. But they-- they got us in, and they-- oy, how do you say it? They-- Were they able to block the entrance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Closed-- blocked with the entrance, with blocks. You know, they-- they set it up like that. And so it was sort of safe for a day or two. But how many days can you live like that, with no air, with no food, with no water? So I think we survived, I don't know, a few days. I can't remember how many, but some days. But then we had to get out. There was no way you could-- you will die there, or you die here. It's the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father and your brother at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and my two brothers, they stayed in the house, and they were taken out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they died with everybody else. But me and my mother and my sister, we survived a few days. But then they-- they opened this place, and we all got out. And as soon as somebody-- it was at night, but I don't know how they noticed. As soon as they got out, we heard shots. People they start-- the Ukrainians start shooting, shooting. And I think they got everybody. But I managed-- I don't know how I did it. I think it was a miracle. I went under a bush somewhere, and they didn't see me. They walked past me, and they didn't see me. And I was laying there. Then when I heard it quiet down, they're all gone, I crawled into a barn. There was a barn not far from there. And I laid there on the haystack. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Early morning, somebody came in to get the horses, and they saw me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A man, a Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he didn't say a word to me, but I knew what he's going to do. But I have no choice. I mean, I can't run. Where am I going to run? So I stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sent in a couple of militia men, Ukrainians. And they took me to-- that was a Sunday. They took me to the sort of main town. The police station was there. They took me there. And there were a few other Jews that they caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they went around, and whoever they caught, the next day, they shot them. But because it was a Sunday, they weren't going to do the job on Sunday. They were drinking and having a good time. So we was standing there all day.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the evening-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must tell you something funny. Not so funny, but tragic. One of the militia men said to me, do you want to run away? Go. I'll let you go. So I thought, well, I'll try it. So I start running, you know, walking a little bit. So another one got out and caught me, brought me back. That was just a play for them. Anyway, that night, they brought us into the militia in the basement. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when I got in there, I saw mine dad's-- like a jacket that he was wearing, and I recognized that it was his. So he must have been there too. And-- But somebody else was wearing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you see the jacket?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the basement there where they took us in, like we were prisoners, right? Where they took us in, I saw mine dad's jacket. And I was sure that he must have been there too. They had him in that same basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was somebody else wearing the jacket?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just laying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just laying there. [DRINKS] And they were-- I was the only girl there, woman. There were more-- the other people were men, and, you know, young, strong men. And they were plotting to get away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I want to go with you too. They said, no, you can't run. You're too young. You haven't got-- and you don't have any shoes on. But I said, no, I want to go to whatever happens. I'll be shot in the back, but I don't want to face them. So they took out a few-- there was sort of a little window. And they took out a few blocks, cement blocks or something, enough to get through. And they started running. And there were four men, and I was the girl that ran too. And as soon as we started running, we heard they start shooting. And they killed all those men. Would you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was the only one-- they didn't want to take me-- that I survived that ordeal. And I kept on running, and running in the middle of the night. I didn't know where I was running. I just knew the direction where I was going. But I didn't know what it was there or anything. I ran and ran until about early hours of the morning. I found myself in front of a house. And not knowing what else to do, I knocked on the door. And I thought maybe they'll have pity on me. And as it turned out, they did. They let me in. They-- they saw who I was. They let me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Ukrainians. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. They gave me something to eat. And they-- they heard. I mean, it was close. How much can you run? A few kilometers-- I mean, I couldn't run who knows how far. So they know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they let me have something to eat. And they said, we can't do for you anything. But you should go out into the woods that were in that area. And they said, there must be other Jews there, and maybe you'll hook up with somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- maybe, maybe. It was nothing concrete. Nobody could tell you what to do. So I had to go. Of course, I went. I was grateful for what they did for me. And I went into the woods there. And I-- there was a couple with a child from another town. But they made it up to this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to bring you back. That family, the Ukrainian family that helped you, do you know their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm sorry. I don't know. I think in those days, I did. But I don't remember. And I-- when I saw this couple, I-- I was really overcome with grief. But at the same time, I was happy to have a soul to talk to, to say a word to. And I stayed with them most of the day until it was evening. They said, you know, the baby cries, because the baby's hungry. And it was a baby, maybe a year old. And they said, for your own good, I think you should move on, not stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because maybe you're by yourself, maybe something will happen. But with us, you have no chance. So I went, and I went further. I mean, where would you go? But I went a little further into the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there-- and I was sitting there, and it was towards evening already. and there was a Ukrainian girl walking with two cows. And she saw me, and she of course knew who I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she went up to me, and she said how sorry she is. And she said, you know what? I'm going to ask my mother if I can bring you home for the night. I mean, she looked very sincere. What language did she speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian. And-- But I mean, I have no-- what can I say? What can I do? I took it as a nice gesture. And she went, and she came back. And she said the mother allowed her to bring me home. And she took me home, and I was with them for at least a month. They kept me in a room. They used to give me food. They used to come in and talk to me. And in the evening, I used to go out to their kitchen and sat with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they risk their life by keeping you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. They definitely did risk their life. But they was very good people. And they lost-- she-- they lost their father and a son to the Soviets. They took them away to Siberia, because I guess they were wealthy farmers. So she was so-- very religious too. And she was just feeling sorry for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the name. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the girl's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the girl, I had her identity after. They gave me her certificate. I think it was a birth certificate. They came up with-- I mean, I could-- one day, a few German soldiers drove up to the house, and they went into the house. And I was in the house with another woman. I don't know what they came for, but I was-- got very scared, and I ran out through the window into the field. They didn't see me. Nothing happened. But it was a warning that you can't stay there any longer, for them and for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came up with a plan. They had a prisoner of war there working for them. And he was from deep Siberia, somewhere from the-- he was a yellow skin, not a white person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- I don't if they bought him or whatever, but he kept quiet. And he was agreeable to take me with him to friends into Russia, because this was all on the Russian border. So we-- and so they told me that that's what they think I should do, and that might help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, we went out in the morning. They gave me some clothes to wear. I had-- I never even had clothes. They gave me some old clothes to wear and a bit of food, and we set out on foot to go to this town. Did you have shoes at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me something to wear, some old boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that false identity, they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was what you-- what was the name on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was Raya Terpelec, the girl's name. And so I had something. You know, it wasn't much, but it was something. And at that time, I took on a gentile identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a Ukrainian girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the name. And I walked with him, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fluent in Ukrainian? Did you feel comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I felt very comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I was blonde-- another thing, I know I was very blond, and blue eyes and fair skin. And I don't think so I looked a typical Jewish person, you know, I could have gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt comfortable with that identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I could. I mean, I was fluent in Ukrainian. I knew Russian a bit. I knew Polish, of course. So languages were no problem. So anyway, we walked, set out walking. We walked a fair distance. And then, because he was wearing this army uniform, and he was an army man, some of the trucks that when they were all-- mostly Russian-- you know, the Germans took in the prison, prisoners driving trucks. So they gave him a ride. And they brought us into this town. It's called Novograd-Volynskii.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- at the time, it was already German, I guess. But it was in the Russian, of course. And we went there to-- they let us off somewhere outside of the city, and we walked up. He had friends there, this fellow. So he said he'll take me there. And we went there, and they let me stay the night over there. They fed us supper. And the next morning, he was going back where he came from, and I was on my own. And I started walking the streets without knowing what to do. I mean, those people, they didn't have it much themselves, and they wasn't going to keep me there. I mean, they didn't know that I was Jewish anymore. I wasn't already.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they weren't going to keep me. They couldn't afford it. So I was walking the streets of this town, like in the market or something. And what do I do? What am I going to do? It's getting dark, and where am I going to go? What am I going to do? And all of a sudden, I notice there's a-- a row of people, a-- A line-up?

speaker: interviewee
text: A line-up. People are lined up. And there are-- well, I think there are freight cars. And I thought to myself, maybe that will be my rescue. Maybe that's what I should do. Because we were talking about things like that. We knew that they are taking Ukrainians, Poles to work in Germany. So I understood that that must be the thing. But I mean, nobody came and said, I want to go with you. I mean, they caught them and brought them, and they all went against their will. But I thought, well, I'll just stand in the line. They don't know. And I stood in the line, and I was going. And they took me. I had presented them with this piece of paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they challenge you when you presented them with the piece of paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. What do they care? It's a person with two hands, and maybe they'll need one of those. They took me in. And-- and the train got all filled up with-- full of people. But it wasn't like the trains that took people to Auschwitz, I'm telling you. Because some Gentiles want to sort of equate themselves with what happened to Jews. It was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't a cattle car. It was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was freight train. Some had benches, some didn't. But they gave them food. They treated them more or less like human beings. But we were not treated like humans anywhere, nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't think I remember, because it was day and night, till we went back, like, didn't go into Russia, but went back to Poland and into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they fed you on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- you know, rations. Everything was rationed in those days. But they give you something to keep you alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread, probably, some sort of coffee and maybe soup, you know. Not great stuff. And we arrived in this city. It used to be Poland before, Katowice. But now, of course, it was German. And actually, Katowice is not far from this camp that we talk so much about, Auschwitz. It's in that area. And this part of Poland had a lot of folks, Germans. They were Germans from before the war. Then they were Polish when it was Poland. And now they came back to be Germans. They were vicious people. That's why they had all the camps in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got there, they took you to what's called a [NON-ENGLISH]. They took you there, and then they assigned you to different places to work. I was assigned to a-- to a couple. They were butchers. They had a butcher shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think they were well-off. There was a pretty nice lady, and he was a big fat German-looking German. And they got me there, but I must say that they weren't too bad to me. They were, you know-- I mean, there was food. There was a decent place to sleep. They used to get help, like cleaning ladies to come in, and this cleaning lady was telling me all the stories about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Polish, it's called the [POLISH]. And she kept on telling me-- and [NON-ENGLISH] how do you say it? She crossed herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: She crossed herself for all the Jews that are dying there. And she had been telling me all the stories. And she doesn't know who I am, who she's telling it, how much it hurts me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she know about what was happening to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was near. I mean, word got around. They knew what was happening. It's not that they didn't know. Everybody knew what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I stayed with those people. I worked there. I worked in the butcher shop. I worked in the house and everything else. I did-- I think I missed something to tell you, and I'm going back to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being at that train going to Germany, all of a sudden, I see a friend of mine on that train. And she ran away with Aryan papers before they liquidated the ghetto, and she was with some Ukrainians living. But then of course, you know, they couldn't. And she got the same idea, and she came on that same train. And I can't tell you, when I saw her. You know--
